preamble: In English and other Germanic languages, the voiceless bilabial stop /p/ is aspirated [pʰ] in syllable-initial position and unaspirated [p] after /s/ or in unstressed syllables. This is a textbook case of allophony: the two phonetic variants are in complementary distribution, conditioned entirely by position within the syllable. English speakers typically do not notice the difference because the aspiration is never phonemically contrastive. Note: in Hindi, Mandarin, and other languages, /p/ and /pʰ/ are distinct phonemes — not allophones — and that contrast is separate from the English allophonic pattern described here.

examples.note: 'pit' [pʰɪt] vs. 'spit' [spɪt]: the same phoneme /p/ is aspirated word-initially but unaspirated after /s/. Complementary allophones.
Standard German aspirates voiceless stops word-initially: 'Paar' [pʰaːɐ] 'pair' vs. unaspirated in consonant clusters.
Included for contrast: in Thai, [p] and [pʰ] are separate phonemes forming a minimal pair — the opposite of the English allophonic pattern.

sources: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics. Harcourt.
Roach, P. (2009). English Phonetics and Phonology. Cambridge University Press.
context: Syllable-initial before stressed vowel = aspirated [pʰ]; post-/s/ = unaspirated [p]
